title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Water
description: Curated OT/ICS cybersecurity advisories, incidents and threat intelligence from primary sources. Editorial summaries by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

_otthreatfeed.usage: Free to reuse with attribution. Credit 'Mike Holcomb & UtilSec' and link back to otthreatfeed.com and to each item's linked primary source. Summaries are editorial paraphrase - verify against the primary source before acting.

items.title: Federal alert sharpens: Iran-affiliated hackers are actively manipulating water-system controllers
items.content_text: Federal agencies escalated their warning that Iran-affiliated actors are actively manipulating the controllers that run US water and wastewater systems. Advisory AA26-097A, updated 22 July, confirms hackers have reached internet-exposed PLCs — Unitronics, Rockwell and others — extracted project files, and rewritten or deleted control logic, in some cases disabling shutdown and alarm functions so equipment can drift into unsafe states without alerting operators. For a small or mid-size utility this is the sharpest version of a familiar problem: the devices being hit are ordinary dosing, pump and lift-station controllers left reachable from the internet with default or shared passwords. The campaign has already caused operational disruption across the sector. The fix is unglamorous — pull controllers behind a firewall, replace default credentials, and require MFA on every remote path.

items.title: FSB Center 16 router campaign (AA26-194A): exposed edge gear in front of municipal water SCADA
items.content_text: A joint advisory from NSA, CISA, the FBI and ~12 partner nations (AA26-194A, 13 July) details how Russia’s FSB Center 16 — Static Tundra / Berserk Bear / Energetic Bear / Dragonfly — scans for devices exposing SNMP with default community strings and abuses Cisco Smart Install to copy device configs. State and local government is named explicitly, which is what most municipal water utilities are. The same internet-facing edge routers sit in front of treatment-plant and lift-station SCADA, and a stolen config is a ready-made map before an intruder touches a controller.

items.title: Lantronix serial-to-IP converter flaw exploited in the wild — the forgotten boxes bridging pump and dosing gear
items.content_text: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2025-67038 in Lantronix serial-to-IP converters, disclosed via the BRIDGE:BREAK research. For water utilities this is an awkward class of device: serial-to-IP converters are how legacy pump controllers, chlorine analysers, level sensors and remote lift-station gear reached Ethernet — usually installed by an integrator, never inventoried, never patched. Compromising the bridge gives access to whatever sits behind it. Exploitation is confirmed and follows an explicit OT threat warning, so the honest first step is discovery, not patching.

items.title: “Boiling Point”: state actors treat US water systems as a standing strategic target
items.content_text: A DomainTools “Boiling Point” analysis frames the strategic picture behind the tactical alerts: Iran-, Russia- and China-aligned actors have deliberately elevated water and wastewater systems from opportunistic nuisance to a standing strategic target across 2024–2026, with Russia-aligned groups assessed as most willing to directly manipulate water controls. The payoff is largely psychological and political — water sits at the intersection of public health and public trust. The exploited weaknesses are the ordinary ones: exposed HMIs/PLCs, default accounts, legacy controllers and thin monitoring.

items.title: FortiSandbox RCE flaws added to CISA KEV — exploited appliances on the utility IT/OT boundary
items.content_text: CISA added two critical Fortinet flaws, CVE-2026-39808 and CVE-2026-25089 (both CVSS 9.1), to the KEV catalog on 16 July after evidence of in-the-wild exploitation. They are OS command-injection bugs in FortiSandbox / Cloud / PaaS allowing unauthenticated command execution via crafted HTTP requests. Fortinet appliances often sit on the boundary between a utility’s IT and its plant OT; a compromised inspection appliance is both a foothold and a blind spot. CISA set a 19 July federal remediation deadline.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Added to KEV 16 Jul 2026 — actively exploited

items.title: OpenPLC v3 advisory flagged for water & wastewater systems
items.content_text: On 9 July CISA published an advisory covering OpenPLC v3, explicitly flagged for Water and Wastewater Systems as well as Energy. OpenPLC is an open-source soft-PLC used in small deployments, labs and some production settings; where it runs unmanaged or internet-reachable it inherits the same exposure risks as any controller. No exploitation is reported, but any water utility running OpenPLC should treat this as a patch-and-isolate item.

items.title: EPA’s 2026 National Cyber Drill tests whether water still flows when the network doesn’t
items.content_text: The EPA launched its 2026 National Cybersecurity Drill challenging drinking-water and wastewater utilities to keep critical functions running when telecom and internet access are degraded or gone. The scenario forces operations in a “disconnected” state — limited or no SCADA remote connectivity, cloud services, email or VoIP — to test manual fallback and sector resilience. Participation is a low-cost way to pressure-test whether a utility can run blind.

items.title: Congress temporarily extends water cyber authorities as EPA issues new guidance
items.content_text: Congress temporarily extended lapsing water-sector cybersecurity legal authorities while the EPA released new guidance for utilities, keeping the federal support structure in place amid ongoing debate over a permanent regulatory model for water. For utilities the practical signal is continuity: baseline expectations and available federal resources remain, and permanent requirements may still be coming.
